ground_truth.title: Adventures in the land of Language Servers
ground_truth.author: Jakub Kozłowski
ground_truth.main_content:
Have you ever wondered how your editors and IDEs are able to support so many programming languages? Perhaps you've been thinking about designing your own language and wanted to know how you can give it editor support? This talk is for you - I've spent over a year building a small language and integrating it with code editors, and I'd like to share some of the challenges I've faced, as well as lessons I've learned in that time. I'll also show how easy it is to build a new Language Server project in Scala 3 thanks to the Langoustine library.

The presentation follows the story of Jane, a developer tasked with building a language DSL for a JVM-based Scala application. After completing the compiler, users requested editor support including analysis features (error highlighting, go-to-definition), refactoring capabilities (rename, remove unused), and completions. Jane initially integrates with IntelliJ using its Java-based API, but when users request VS Code support, she faces challenges with Node.js and JVM interoperability.

Jane develops a client-server architecture with a language backend running on the JVM and frontends for different editors. She creates a protocol for communication called the Language Backend Protocol. When Trish from another team wants to support a different language with different features, Jane realizes the need for optional server capabilities that servers can advertise to clients.

Addressing user feedback about slow operations and lack of feedback, Jane switches to JSON-RPC for bidirectional communication, enabling servers to send intermediate results and notifications back to clients. The protocol evolves to handle unsaved files through text synchronization methods, caching, and pre-computing analysis results.

The story concludes with Jane open-sourcing the protocol at Microsoft around 2015, revealing this is the origin story of the Language Server Protocol (LSP).
with: Have you ever wondered how your editors and IDEs are able to support so many programming languages?
Jane is tasked with building a language... A small DSL meant to be used in an existing application
The protocol needs to account for unsaved files - the protocol identifies files by their disk path
Jane wants to open source the protocol - the idea is generic enough to handle many more languages
This is the origin story of the Language Server Protocol (LSP)
